moment_text: Sure. You know, I think about this question a lot because I always wonder how did I end up here? When I first went to medical school, I thought I was going to be an OB GYN, right? So I said, okay, that that's clearly not the path for me. And so I was doing my different clinical rotations and they were all exciting, but I was trying to figure out what spoke to me. And then I did an adolescent medicine rotation under my pediatrics and I said, this is great. Oh my gosh, teenagers, they're so much fun. I love this. And I was prepared to apply to pediatrics. moment_text: So you go into psychiatry, talk about that journey, um, into that part of your medical training. Fortunately, I had a good foundational experience with mental health and psychology even before I got to medical school. Right. I thought I was going to have this beautiful office in Towson, Maryland, which is one of the suburbs in Baltimore. And I was going to do deep analytic work because again, what I liked about this was getting really deep into the lives of people. But my first rotation in our, one of our state psychiatric hospitals, Carter Centers called Carter Center, it's now closed, helped me to see that there were individuals with severe mental illness in very difficult life circumstances that also had very rich stories. moment_text: And so lemme, I guess, do a little bit of definition first, because a lot of people may not know what we mean when we say community psychiatry. My understanding of community psychiatry is working with people, many of whom have severe and persistent mental illness. Things like schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, very severe depressions, so individuals with severe, severe and persistent mental illness that are living often in complicated social environments. Right. Urban environments, impoverished environments. So you have the marriage of the complexity of mental illness, which we are still learning about, and the complexity of social factors and, and what a community psychiatrist does is we try to get in there and say, okay, how can I help this person live their best life with the much, as much joy and happiness as they can have given the factors at play? Right. So as a senior psychiatrist, it's not necessarily. My goal to eliminate all of the symptoms, lemme not say that it is my goal. It is my goal. It may not always be possible, but that doesn't mean that people can't have wonderful joy and fulfillment and and enriching lives.

moment_text: So as a community psychiatrist, that's always been my mission and I've done that in various ways. Working with people, you know, chicken coops in the middle of the woods, Baltimore Blue Lights where the police are you, that, that's the kind of setting that we practiced in. So it, it was just great. You really were meeting people where they were and when you meet people where they are, which is interesting because that's translated into other parts of my career. moment_text: And, you know, it's a false, um, separation to say, you know, we've got individuals with illness and then there's these systems issues. They are so interwoven. And what I learned in that setting was we had two types of individuals. One group who had just that kind of organic, severe mental illness that helped lead to their disenfranchisement. But then we also have people who were disenfranchised that the disenfranchisement led to mental illness and mental health concerns, and so they're so interwoven. If you have people living in poverty and you have people with limited or no social supports and you have people without access to resources or, or self-efficacy, it's, it's almost a given that they're going to be depressed. That they're going to be anxious, that they're going to have panic attacks, that they're going to have what we like to call mood swings, which is a whole array of different things.

moment_text: I would say first there's a couple different things. You're not alone. Whatever you're feeling and experiencing, whatever your circumstances may be, you are not alone. Okay? Even if there's no one around you, trust, you're not alone. And it's okay to say, you know what? I need a little bit of help, need a little bit of support. It's okay to express your needs. And then it's okay to figure out how to get them next.

moment_text: It's that I just wanna give women permission to be, and it's okay. It's okay to be, and there are other women out there like you and, and, and not just women. You know, I, if my husband were here, he'd say, I'm Kayla too. Yes, there are wonderful men that care as well. But find your community, find your tribe, find your supports. And it's, it's a okay, you have, you have permission.
